question.text: Which of the following is a characteristic of an open-ended mutual fund?

answer.text: Investors can buy and sell units at NAV-linked prices on any business day
Units are issued only during the New Fund Offer period
The fund has a fixed maturity date when units are compulsorily redeemed
Units can only be bought and sold on stock exchanges

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** An open-ended mutual fund does not have a fixed maturity date. Investors can buy additional units from the fund at any time at NAV-linked prices, and existing investors can sell their units back to the fund at current NAV-linked prices. It may also be listed on stock exchanges.
**The Trap:** The other options describe closed-ended funds — units issued only during NFO, fixed maturity date, and mandatory stock exchange listing. Open-ended funds allow continuous purchase and redemption directly with the fund.
**Cross-Exam Context:** MF topics overlap with Series V-A, V-B. The distinction between open-ended and closed-ended structures is a fundamental concept across all MF exams.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Most retail investors prefer open-ended funds because of the liquidity — they can redeem on any business day. Highlight this flexibility when recommending funds, but also explain that exit loads may apply for early redemptions.
